react.answer: ## Building a Lazy-loaded Image in React **Mental model:** Don't set the image `src` until the element is about to enter the viewport. An `IntersectionObserver` watches a wrapper; on first intersection you assign the real `src` (and can stop observing). A placeholder shows until the image's `onLoad` fires, then you fade it in. This saves bandwidth for off-screen images. ### Step 1 — Observe the wrapper ```jsx const ref = useRef(null); const [visible, setVisible] = useState(false); useEffect(() => { const io = new IntersectionObserver(([e]) => { if (e.isIntersecting) { setVisible(true); io.disconnect(); } }, { rootMargin: '100px' }); if (ref.current) io.observe(ref.current); return () => io.disconnect(); }, []); ``` ### Step 2 — Swap src only when visible ```jsx setLoaded(true)} /> ``` ### Step 3 — Placeholder + fade-in Keep a low-cost placeholder behind the image; fade the image's opacity once `loaded`. ```jsx ``` ### Step 4 — rootMargin for prefetch A positive `rootMargin` (e.g. `100px`) starts loading just before the image scrolls in, hiding the latency. **Edge cases** | Case | Handling | | :-- | :-- | | Already in view | Observer fires immediately on mount. | | Layout shift | Reserve the image's box (width/height) up front. | | No IO support | Fall back to native `loading="lazy"`. | **Interview tip:** Mention that the platform now has `` for the common case — the `IntersectionObserver` version is what you reach for when you need custom thresholds, fade-ins, or to lazy-load non-img content.

vue.answer: ## Building a Lazy-loaded Image in Vue 3 **Mental model:** Don't bind the image `src` until the element nears the viewport. An `IntersectionObserver` on the wrapper sets `visible` on first intersection (then disconnects). A placeholder shows until `@load` fires; then fade the image in. Saves bandwidth for off-screen images. ### Step 1 — Observe the wrapper ```js const root = ref(null); const visible = ref(false); onMounted(() => { io = new IntersectionObserver(([e]) => { if (e.isIntersecting) { visible.value = true; io.disconnect(); } }, { rootMargin: '100px' }); io.observe(root.value); }); onUnmounted(() => io && io.disconnect()); ``` ### Step 2 — Bind src only when visible ```vue ``` ### Step 3 — Placeholder + fade-in ```vue ``` ### Step 4 — rootMargin for prefetch `rootMargin: '100px'` starts loading just before it scrolls in. **Edge cases** | Case | Handling | | :-- | :-- | | Already visible | Observer fires on mount. | | Layout shift | Reserve the box dimensions. | | No IO | Fall back to `loading="lazy"`. | **Interview tip:** Note native `loading="lazy"` covers the simple case; the observer approach wins when you need a fade-in, custom thresholds, or to lazy-load arbitrary content.

angular.answer: ## Building a Lazy-loaded Image in Angular **Mental model:** Don't bind the image `src` until the element nears the viewport. An `IntersectionObserver` (set up in `ngAfterViewInit`) sets `visible` on first intersection, then disconnects. A placeholder shows until `(load)` fires; then fade in. Classic `NgModule` setup, no `?.`/`??` (the build rejects them). ### Step 1 — Observe the host ```typescript @ViewChild('frame') frame!: ElementRef; visible = false; ngAfterViewInit(): void { this.io = new IntersectionObserver(([e]) => { if (e.isIntersecting) { this.visible = true; this.io.disconnect(); } }, { rootMargin: '100px' }); this.io.observe(this.frame.nativeElement); } ``` ### Step 2 — Bind src only when visible ```html ``` ### Step 3 — Placeholder + fade-in ```html ``` ### Step 4 — rootMargin + cleanup `rootMargin: '100px'` prefetches; disconnect in `ngOnDestroy`. **Edge cases** | Case | Handling | | :-- | :-- | | Already visible | Observer fires on init. | | Layout shift | Reserve the box dimensions. | | No IO | Fall back to `loading="lazy"`. | **Interview tip:** Set up the observer in `ngAfterViewInit` (the `@ViewChild` node exists then) and disconnect in `ngOnDestroy`; mention native `loading="lazy"` as the simple-case alternative.
